does: This paper establishes quantitative scaling laws for agent systems by empirically measuring how performance changes across key dimensions including model capability, coordination mechanisms, and task characteristics. The authors conduct controlled experiments across 260 configurations spanning six agentic benchmarks and five agent architectures (single-agent and four multi-agent variants: Independent, Centralized, Decentralized, and Hybrid) using three LLM families. They develop a predictive model that achieves cross-validated R²=0.373 across all benchmarks and R²=0.413 using task-grounded capability metrics. The work identifies critical patterns including a robust capability-saturation effect, diminishing returns from coordination beyond certain single-agent performance thresholds, and overhead costs in multi-agent systems for tool-heavy tasks by systematically isolating architectural effects through standardized tools, prompts, and compute budgets.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of multi-agent systems and reinforcement learning coordination literature by applying modern scaling analysis techniques developed for language models to agentic architectures. It extends prior work on agent benchmarking and capability evaluation by introducing systematic scaling principles similar to those discovered for language model pretraining. The paper responds to the gap between widespread adoption of language model agents in practice and limited theoretical understanding of their performance characteristics, drawing connections to classical multi-agent coordination research while grounding analysis in contemporary large-scale language model systems. It complements work on prompt engineering, tool-use in language models, and architectural designs for reasoning systems by providing empirical relationships between system design choices and measured outcomes.
enables: These scaling principles provide a foundation for predicting agent system performance under new configurations without exhaustive retraining, enabling more efficient system design and resource allocation for practitioners building real-world applications. The identified patterns around coordination overhead and capability saturation point toward future research into more efficient multi-agent architectures and selective deployment strategies that activate coordination only when beneficial. The work establishes methodology for systematically studying agent system design space, supporting the development of theoretical frameworks that explain why these scaling patterns emerge. It opens opportunities to optimize agent system composition, investigate whether identified principles hold across broader task distributions, and develop predictive models that guide decisions about when to scale model capability versus coordination complexity.
